title: Bare-metal vs RTOS trade-offs
description: A super-loop is simple and deterministic but scales poorly; an RTOS adds preemption and structure at the cost of footprint and concurrency hazards.
body: Use when choosing the firmware architecture for a device. A bare-metal super-loop with interrupts is tiny and easy to reason about for a few tasks, but timing couples as it grows; an RTOS gives preemptive priorities and blocking primitives at the cost of RAM/flash and new hazards (priority inversion, stack-per-task, races). Do not add an RTOS reflexively when a state machine would do. Actions: match the model to the number of independent deadlines and concurrency needs, and size RAM for per-task stacks. Validation signals include meeting all deadlines with the chosen model under stress. Risk boundary: an RTOS misused (everything one priority, blocking in ISRs) is worse than a clean super-loop; if real-time guarantees are unclear, escalate for a timing review before committing the architecture.
